preFix.title: Fitting a newel post to stairs
preFix.subtitle: Secure a loose or replacement newel post at the foot of the stair
preFix.excerpt: How to fix a newel post securely at the base of a stair: understanding how newel posts are fixed to the stringer and floor, drilling and bolting the post, and checking for plumb before fastening the handrail.
preFix.sourceNotes: Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: is a safety issue. The post carries the load of anyone gripping the handrail on the stair; it must not rock or twist. The traditional fixing is a through-bolt into the
end, supplemented by adhesive or timber blocking. A post that moves at the base indicates a failed joint or a deteriorated stringer, not just a loose screw.

A 12 mm or 16 mm spade bit or auger bit, a corresponding coach bolt with nut and washer (typically M10 or M12, 150 mm long), a socket set or spanner for the nut, a spirit level, a chisel set, and wood adhesive. If the stringer end is damaged, timber blocking from matching wood and wood glue.

Grip the post and rock it firmly in all directions. Movement front-to-back indicates the bolted connection to the stringer has failed. Movement side-to-side on an older stair often means the post was never properly bolted but only relying on the skirt boarding for lateral stability. Inspect the joint between the post base and the stringer end; tap for hollow sounds indicating a gap.
Step 2, remove and repair the joint area

Remove any skirt board or timber blocking to expose the post and stringer junction. If the stringer end is split or compressed at the post joint, cut away the damaged material and glue-in a timber patch from matching timber, clamping for at least 8 hours before continuing.
Step 3, drill and bolt through the stringer

Drill a hole through the stringer end and into the post at the centre of the post section, angled slightly upward so the bolt head is accessible from the side of the stringer. Insert a coach bolt, washer, and nut; tighten fully with the spanner. For added stability, drive two 100 mm screws from the stringer into the post at 45 degrees above and below the coach bolt line.
Step 4, check for plumb and refix trims

Check the post is plumb on two faces with the spirit level before the final tightening of the nut. Once confirmed plumb, tighten, refit the skirt board, and check the handrail connection at the top of the post. The handrail is typically dowelled or housed into the post cap; if it has worked loose, apply wood adhesive and cramp until set.

attrs.fix: Pack the gap with a hardwood shim and wood glue before tightening the bolt.
attrs.cause: Gap between the post base and the stringer face; the bolt is the only contact point.

attrs.fix: Add packing behind the post base on the appropriate face to correct the lean before final tightening.
attrs.cause: Stringer end was not perpendicular; the post follows the face it is bolted to.
attrs.symptom: Post out of plumb after fixing

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
